To find the characteristic polynomial of the matrix $A$, we need to compute the determinant of the matrix $A - \lambda I$, where $I$ is the identity matrix and $\lambda$ is a scalar variable.

The given matrix is:
$$ A = \begin{pmatrix} 12 & -1 & -12 & 9 \\ -13 & -7 & 13 & 2 \\ -3 & -3 & 13 & -14 \\ -13 & 14 & 3 & -10 \end{pmatrix} $$

The characteristic polynomial is defined as $p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)$.
$$ A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} 12-\lambda & -1 & -12 & 9 \\ -13 & -7-\lambda & 13 & 2 \\ -3 & -3 & 13-\lambda & -14 \\ -13 & 14 & 3 & -10-\lambda \end{pmatrix} $$

Calculating the determinant of a $4 \times 4$ matrix directly is tedious. Let's look for simplifications using row or column operations to create zeros, or check if the matrix has specific properties (like integer eigenvalues).

Let's try to find the eigenvalues by testing small integer values for $\lambda$. The constant term of the characteristic polynomial is $\det(A)$. The coefficient of $\lambda^3$ is $-\text{tr}(A)$.
The trace of $A$ is the sum of the diagonal elements:
$$ \text{tr}(A) = 12 + (-7) + 13 + (-10) = 12 - 7 + 13 - 10 = 8 $$
So, the polynomial will be of the form $p(\lambda) = \lambda^4 - 8\lambda^3 + c_2\lambda^2 + c_1\lambda + c_0$.
